| 757 | 2026-03-15 | 11.418 support slice | DONE | Closed the stale-replica `allow-stale` scripting/function parity gap. Added exact TCP integration regressions `eval_allow_stale_matches_external_stale_replica_semantics` and `function_allow_stale_matches_external_stale_replica_semantics`, plus a processor-level stale-replica wait helper for deterministic replication-state observation. Implemented explicit stale-replica semantics in the scripting runtime: `EVAL`/`EVALSHA`/`FCALL`/`FCALL_RO` now reject top-level execution with `MASTERDOWN` unless the shebang/function metadata includes `allow-stale`, and nested `redis.call`/`redis.pcall` now deny non-`CMD_STALE`-equivalent commands on stale replicas with `ERR Can not execute the command on a stale replica` while still permitting `ECHO`, `INFO`, `TIME`, and `SLOWLOG GET|LEN|HELP`. Also fixed the connection-layer pre-dispatch gates so scripting commands are classified by effective flags rather than raw command ids: `EVAL`/`EVALSHA` no-writes shebangs no longer trip `READONLY`, `allow-stale` scripts/functions get `MASTERDOWN` precedence on stale replicas, and `SCRIPT LOAD` is treated as replica-safe / stale-safe cache admin instead of a write. Validation: targeted Rust PASS (`cargo test -p garnet-server eval_allow_stale_matches_external_stale_replica_semantics -- --nocapture`, `cargo test -p garnet-server function_allow_stale_matches_external_stale_replica_semantics -- --nocapture`); targeted external probe attempt via `redis_runtest_external_subset.sh` confirmed the upstream cases remain marked `external:skip` (`allow-stale shebang flag: Not supported on external server`); `make fmt` PASS; `make test-server` PASS (`653 passed; 0 failed; 4 ignored`, `23 passed`, `1 passed`). |
| 758 | 2026-03-15 | 11.503 support slice | DONE | Landed the first real ACL runtime slice around ordinary Redis authentication. Moved ACL user state out of `ServerMetrics` and onto `RequestProcessor`, expanded `AclUserProfile` to carry password hashes / `nopass` / command and channel state, and implemented command-path `AUTH` plus `HELLO ... AUTH username password` verification with connection-auth side effects applied only after successful replies. `ACL WHOAMI`, `ACL USERS`, `ACL LIST`, `ACL SETUSER`, `ACL DELUSER`, and `ACL GETUSER` now read and mutate live ACL user state instead of returning fixed stubs, including password add/remove/hash forms and hashed-password redaction in `ACL GETUSER`. Added exact TCP regression `acl_auth_password_rotation_and_hello_auth_match_external_scenarios`, mirroring the early Redis `unit/acl.tcl` password rotation flow (`>password`, `<password`, `#hash`, `!hash`, disabled-user rejection, `HELLO AUTH`, and `WHOAMI`). Validation: `make check` PASS; targeted cargo PASS (`acl_auth_password_rotation_and_hello_auth_match_external_scenarios`, `reset_clears_multi_and_authenticated_state`, `acl_cat_returns_full_category_list_and_getuser_returns_default_profile`, `server_admin_commands_cover_auth_select_move_swapdb_client_role_wait_and_save_family`); targeted external probe `RUNTEXT_EXTRA_ARGS='--single unit/acl' ./redis_runtest_external_subset.sh` completed with upstream `external:skip` coverage only (`ok=0 err=0 timeout=0 ignore=8`); `make fmt` PASS; `make test-server` PASS (`654 passed; 0 failed; 4 ignored`, `23 passed`, `1 passed`). `11.503` remains active for richer ACL enforcement parity (channels, selectors, pubsub subscriber revocation/pardon, and command/key/channel enforcement). |
| 759 | 2026-03-15 | 11.503 support slice | DONE | Landed the next real ACL runtime slice around command/key/channel enforcement and live pubsub state reconciliation. Added top-level connection-path ACL gates so fast-path commands (`CLIENT`, `SELECT`, `MONITOR`, non-MULTI control flow) no longer bypass ACL, and kept a second ACL re-check on resumed blocking commands so a user who loses permissions while blocked is woken with `NOPERM` instead of executing stale intent. Expanded `ACL SETUSER` rule parsing and rendering to support `+@category` / `-@category`, `+command` / `-command`, and first-arg rules like `+client|id`, `-config|set`, and `+select|0`; `ACL CAT <category>` now returns live category members; `ACL DRYRUN` now authorizes against the real runtime profile instead of a stub `+OK`. Added live pubsub subscriber reconciliation on ACL profile updates: when channel permissions are narrowed, only clients whose current channel/pattern subscriptions are no longer permitted are killed; widened or `allchannels` profiles leave valid subscribers intact. Scripting-side `redis.acl_check_cmd` now reuses the same generic ACL evaluator, and `PUBLISH`/`SPUBLISH` channel authorization now scopes only the channel argument, not the payload. Exact Rust regressions were added first for the external scenarios: `acl_command_key_channel_and_dryrun_permissions_match_external_scenarios`, `acl_pubsub_revocation_and_blocking_recheck_match_external_scenarios`, plus refreshed unit coverage for `ACL CAT` and `ACL DRYRUN`. Validation: targeted cargo PASS (`cargo test -p garnet-server acl_ -- --nocapture` => `8 passed; 0 failed`); `make fmt` PASS; `make test-server` PASS (`656 passed; 0 failed; 4 ignored`, `23 passed`, `1 passed`). The upstream external `unit/acl.tcl` lane is still marked `external:skip`, so external verification remains exact Rust TCP coverage until that harness restriction is lifted. |
| 760 | 2026-03-15 | 11.503 support slice | DONE | Landed the next ACL runtime slice around live `ACL LOG`. `AclAuthorizationError` now carries the exact denied key/channel object instead of only the reason, `RequestProcessor` owns an aggregated ACL log ring with Redis-style grouping (`count`, `entry-id`, timestamps, `client-info`) and reason/context tracking (`command`, `key`, `channel`, `auth`; `toplevel`, `multi`, future `lua`/`script`). `AUTH` and `HELLO ... AUTH` wrong-password paths now emit auth log entries, and all connection-path ACL denials now log through a single helper that takes an explicit `AclLogContext`, preserving `multi` context even when denial happens before request-lifecycle execution. `ACL LOG [count]` and `ACL LOG RESET` now return live RESP2/RESP3-compatible payloads instead of empty stubs. Exact TCP regressions added first: `acl_log_tracks_auth_and_acl_denials_like_external_scenarios` and `acl_log_distinguishes_multi_context_like_external_scenario`, mirroring Redis `unit/acl.tcl` auth / toplevel / key / channel / multi cases and verifying aggregation plus RESP3 map shape. Validation: targeted cargo PASS (`cargo test -p garnet-server acl_log_ -- --nocapture`, `cargo test -p garnet-server acl_command_key_channel_and_dryrun_permissions_match_external_scenarios -- --nocapture`); targeted external probe `RUNTEXT_EXTRA_ARGS='--single unit/acl' ./redis_runtest_external_subset.sh` completed with upstream `external:skip` coverage only (`ok=0 err=0 timeout=0 ignore=8`) at `garnet-rs/tests/interop/results/redis-runtest-external-20260315-192016/`; `make fmt` PASS; `make test-server` PASS (`658 passed; 0 failed; 4 ignored`, `23 passed`, `1 passed`). The upstream external `unit/acl.tcl` lane remains `external:skip`, so the authoritative regression for this slice is the exact Rust TCP coverage. |
| 761 | 2026-03-15 | 11.503 support slice | DONE | Landed the next ACL runtime slice inside the scripting runtime. `redis.call` / `redis.pcall` now enforce the current authenticated user's ACL profile before dispatching inner commands, so unauthorized calls fail with canonical `NOPERM` instead of bypassing ACL through `execute_bytes_in_db`. Because the denial now happens while `RequestProcessor` still knows the running script kind, the existing `ACL LOG` machinery starts emitting real `lua` / `script` contexts for `EVAL*` and `FCALL*` inner-command violations. Added exact TCP regression `scripting_acl_enforcement_and_acl_log_context_match_external_scenarios`, which mirrors the Redis ACL shape for denied `EVAL`/`FCALL` inner `INCR` calls and asserts both the client-visible `NOPERM` surface and the resulting `ACL LOG` `context=lua|script`, `reason=command`, `object=incr`, `client-info cmd=eval|fcall` entries. Revalidated the pre-existing `scripting_acl_check_cmd_matches_external_scenario_for_eval_and_function` regression to ensure the new runtime enforcement did not regress `redis.acl_check_cmd(...)`. Validation: targeted cargo PASS (`cargo test -p garnet-server scripting_acl_enforcement_and_acl_log_context_match_external_scenarios -- --nocapture`, `cargo test -p garnet-server scripting_acl_check_cmd_matches_external_scenario_for_eval_and_function -- --nocapture`); targeted external probe `RUNTEXT_EXTRA_ARGS='--single unit/acl' ./redis_runtest_external_subset.sh` remains upstream `external:skip` only (`ok=0 err=0 timeout=0 ignore=8`) at `garnet-rs/tests/interop/results/redis-runtest-external-20260315-192436/`; `make fmt` PASS; `make test-server` PASS (`659 passed; 0 failed; 4 ignored`, `23 passed`, `1 passed`). |

| 763 | 2026-03-15 | 11.503 support slice | DONE | Landed the live `acllog-max-len` config coupling slice. Added `acllog-max-len` to the mutable config surface and default overrides, threaded it into `RequestProcessor` as a runtime atomic cap, and changed `push_acl_log_entry` so `CONFIG SET acllog-max-len 0` suppresses new entries without retroactively clearing existing ones while positive values clamp the live ring length on insertion, matching Redis `unit/acl.tcl`. Added exact TCP regression `acl_log_max_len_config_matches_external_scenarios`, which drives denied `SET` attempts under `acllog-max-len = 0` and `5` and verifies `ACL LOG` lengths `0 -> 5 -> 5` across runtime reconfiguration. Validation: targeted cargo PASS (`cargo test -p garnet-server acl_log_max_len_config_matches_external_scenarios -- --nocapture`, `cargo test -p garnet-server acl_ -- --nocapture` => `13 passed; 0 failed`); targeted external probe `RUNTEXT_EXTRA_ARGS='--single unit/acl' ./redis_runtest_external_subset.sh` remained upstream `external:skip` only (`ok=0 err=0 timeout=0 ignore=8`) at `garnet-rs/tests/interop/results/redis-runtest-external-20260315-194944/`; `make fmt` PASS; `make test-server` hit the known unrelated full-load flake `multidb_active_expire_increments_expired_keys_active_like_external_scenario` (`660 passed; 1 failed; 4 ignored`) after the ACL targeted gate had already passed. |
| 764 | 2026-03-15 | 11.503 support slice | DONE | Landed ACL selectors as first-class runtime state instead of a stub empty array. Split permission state into a root selector plus attached selector list, added `clearselectors` and parenthesized selector parsing with lenient multi-argument merge, rendered non-root selectors in `ACL GETUSER` and `ACL LIST`, and reworked ACL authorization/pubsub reconciliation to use Valkey-style selector OR semantics with denial precedence `database < command < key < channel`. Also fixed related ACL surface gaps touched by the refactor: `nopass` now clears stored password hashes and `ACL LIST` emits password hashes like Valkey. Added exact TCP regression `acl_selectors_match_valkey_semantics_for_or_permissions_and_rendering`, covering merged selector parsing, `ACL GETUSER selectors`, `ACL LIST` parenthesized selector rendering, cross-selector OR permissions, denial precedence, and `clearselectors`. Validation: `cargo check -p garnet-server --tests` PASS; targeted cargo PASS (`cargo test -p garnet-server acl_selectors_match_valkey_semantics_for_or_permissions_and_rendering -- --nocapture`, `cargo test -p garnet-server acl_ -- --nocapture` => `14 passed; 0 failed`); targeted external probe `RUNTEXT_EXTRA_ARGS='--single unit/acl' ./redis_runtest_external_subset.sh` remained upstream `external:skip` only (`ok=0 err=0 timeout=0 ignore=8`) at `garnet-rs/tests/interop/results/redis-runtest-external-20260315-201031/`; `make fmt` PASS; `make test-server` PASS (`662 passed; 0 failed; 4 ignored`, `23 passed`, `1 passed`). |
| 765 | 2026-03-15 | 11.503 support slice | DONE | Added a narrow external-harness visibility override for ACL burn-down. `garnet-rs/tests/interop/redis_runtest_external_subset.sh` now accepts `RUNTEXT_ALLOW_EXTERNAL_SKIP=1` and temporarily patches Redis `tests/support/server.tcl` so `external:skip` can be bypassed for an explicit focused probe while leaving the default external behavior unchanged. Smoke validation: `bash -n garnet-rs/tests/interop/redis_runtest_external_subset.sh` PASS. Focused external probe: `RUNTEXT_RUN_ONLY_ISOLATED_UNIT=unit/acl RUNTEXT_ALLOW_EXTERNAL_SKIP=1 ... ./redis_runtest_external_subset.sh` completed at `garnet-rs/tests/interop/results/redis-runtest-external-20260315-201656/` with `ok=56 err=31 timeout=0 ignore=1`, which turns the previous ACL blind spot into a concrete backlog. The failure split is now explicit: product gaps (`ACL GETUSER` canonical rendering, `HELLO AUTH/SETNAME` precedence, current-client behavior after `ACL SETUSER ... off`, delete-user disconnect semantics, channel/pattern subscription parity, `ACL LOG` EXEC context) plus a second-order harness limitation where external mode still reuses one long-lived server across top-level `start_server` blocks, so ACL file/startup-config sections do not yet model fresh-server semantics. |
| 766 | 2026-03-15 | 11.503 support slice | DONE | Landed the authenticated-session lifecycle ACL slice. `ServerMetrics` client runtime state now tracks whether a connection is actually authenticated instead of conflating `user=default` with implicit authorization; new connections and `RESET` derive their initial auth state from the live default-user profile (`enabled && nopass`), non-bypass commands now return `NOAUTH Authentication required.` when the client is unauthenticated, and existing authenticated sessions continue to authorize against the current ACL rules even if the underlying user is later toggled `off`. `DELUSER` now marks all affected authenticated clients as killed so the current client gets its integer reply first and then disconnects on the next I/O, matching Redis. `HELLO` was rewritten to use last-option-wins parsing for repeated `AUTH` / `SETNAME`, validating and applying only the final occurrence so bad earlier credentials or names do not poison an otherwise valid HELLO chain. Added exact TCP regressions first: `acl_current_connection_and_hello_chain_match_external_scenarios` and `acl_deluser_disconnects_current_client_after_reply_like_external_scenario`, mirroring Redis `unit/acl.tcl` for `default off`, new unauthenticated connections, `HELLO ... AUTH` precedence, `HELLO ... SETNAME` precedence, failed-auth no-side-effect behavior, and `DELUSER` disconnect-after-reply. Validation: targeted cargo PASS (`cargo test -p garnet-server acl_current_connection_and_hello_chain_match_external_scenarios -- --nocapture`, `cargo test -p garnet-server acl_deluser_disconnects_current_client_after_reply_like_external_scenario -- --nocapture`, `cargo test -p garnet-server acl_ -- --nocapture` => `16 passed; 0 failed`); forced external ACL probe improved from `ok=56 err=31 ignore=1` to `ok=64 err=28 timeout=1 ignore=1` at `garnet-rs/tests/interop/results/redis-runtest-external-20260315-203426/`; `make fmt` PASS; `make test-server` on the full lane still hit the known unrelated full-load flake/hang path (`multidb_watch_flush_swapdb_expire_and_discard_match_external_multi_scenarios`, `multidb_active_expire_increments_expired_keys_active_like_external_scenario`, inline pipelining long runner), and each of those three tests was rerun in isolation and passed. |
| 767 | 2026-03-16 | 11.503 support slice | DONE | Closed the remaining focused ACL backlog for the runnable external lane. Product fixes landed across the remaining Redis `unit/acl.tcl` gaps: runtime config/file coupling (`acl-pubsub-default`, `aclfile`, `user`, `ACL SAVE`, `ACL LOAD`, `ACL DRYRUN`, denied-command INFO counters), canonical ACL rendering fixes for root selectors and `ACL LIST`, exact `&*`/`allchannels` semantics for `PSUBSCRIBE`, `EXEC`-time ACL revalidation with `ACL LOG context=multi`, killed-subscriber rechecks before pending pubsub flush after ACL reload, and a read-only-replica exemption for `ACL LOAD`. Also fixed an unrelated full-suite destabilizer uncovered during the ACL burn-down by making INFO memory estimation stop lazily expiring non-zero-DB keys during inspection. The external harness was tightened rather than left over-broad: it now starts Garnet from `REDIS_REPO_ROOT` so relative `CONFIG SET dir` matches Redis runtest expectations, runs most ACL `external:skip` coverage under `RUNTEXT_ALLOW_EXTERNAL_SKIP=1`, but still preserves upstream skips for true multi-server / restart / startup-only cases (`repl`, `restart_server`, direct `exec src/redis-server`). Validation: targeted cargo PASS (`acl_runtime_aclfile_reload_and_metrics_match_external_scenarios`, `acl_load_is_allowed_while_server_is_replica`, `acl_log_distinguishes_multi_context_like_external_scenario`); `bash -n garnet-rs/tests/interop/redis_runtest_external_subset.sh` PASS; `make fmt` PASS; `make test-server` PASS with verified counts (`668 passed; 0 failed; 4 ignored`, plus `23 passed`, plus `1 passed`); focused external ACL probes progressed through `garnet-rs/tests/interop/results/redis-runtest-external-20260316-003347/` (`ok=85 err=2 timeout=0 ignore=1`), `...-004357/` (`ok=94 err=5 timeout=0 ignore=1`), `...-005623/` (`ok=104 err=3 timeout=0 ignore=2`), and final `garnet-rs/tests/interop/results/redis-runtest-external-20260316-010414/` (`ok=105 err=0 timeout=0 ignore=4`). Remaining ignores are now only upstream `external:skip` scenarios that require dedicated fresh-server or restart semantics, not ACL product gaps. |
| 768 | 2026-03-16 | 11.504 / DR-007 | DONE | Completed the first OIDC JWT / workload-identity `AUTH` slice from the ACL baseline. Locked the library choice to `jsonwebtoken` after current-source comparison with `openidconnect`, `josekit`, and newer JWT alternatives; added file-backed JWKS verification plus `auth-oidc-jwks-file`, `auth-oidc-issuer`, `auth-oidc-audience`, `auth-oidc-principal-claim`, and `auth-oidc-algorithms` config controls; and implemented explicit `AUTH __OIDC__ <jwt>` / `HELLO ... AUTH __OIDC__ <jwt>` so JWT auth is opt-in and ordinary ACL usernames are never implicitly treated as token mode selectors. Successful JWT auth now resolves the real ACL principal from the configured claim, requires that ACL user to exist and be enabled, and updates the connection user to that resolved principal. Also reserved `__OIDC__` from ACL usernames and added exact regressions for success/failure/config-validation paths. Validation: targeted Rust PASS (`oidc_auth_bearer_token_and_hello_auth_match_configured_jwks_scenarios`, `oidc_auth_config_validation_rejects_incomplete_or_unsafe_settings`, `acl_current_connection_and_hello_chain_match_external_scenarios`); `make fmt` PASS; `make test-server` PASS with verified counts (`670 passed; 0 failed; 4 ignored`, plus `23 passed`, plus `1 passed`); targeted external `unit/acl` isolated probe PASS at `garnet-rs/tests/interop/results/redis-runtest-external-20260316-022956/` (`failed_tests=0`, `ignore=8`). `DR-007` remains `REQUESTED_WAITING` for remote JWKS/discovery/refresh and broader workload-identity rollout guidance. |
| 769 | 2026-03-16 | DR-007 | DONE | Imported returned OIDC/workload-identity DeepResearch report from `/Users/kazuki-matsuda/Downloads/deep-research-report (19).md` into `docs/performance/oidc-jwt-auth-workload-identity-deepresearch-2026-03-16.md`, added actionable digest `docs/performance/oidc-jwt-auth-workload-identity-notes-2026-03-16.md`, and updated Phase 11B tracker entry `DR-007` to `RECEIVED` (`pending=3`). The returned report validates the existing `jsonwebtoken` + local JWKS first-slice choice and highlights follow-up work for remote discovery/JWKS refresh, `kid`-miss refresh/LKG caching, connection-lifetime `exp` enforcement, and provider-oriented rollout guidance; the digest also records the deliberate divergence that Garnet keeps the explicit `AUTH __OIDC__ <jwt>` sentinel rather than using ordinary ACL usernames as the token-mode selector. |
| 770 | 2026-03-16 | replication design notes | DONE | Captured the current full-sync / cutover design discussion in `docs/performance/replication-fullsync-cutover-notes-2026-03-16.md` before the new DeepResearch results return. The note records the current runtime facts (single global downstream stream/offset, empty-RDB export for real replicas, upstream RDB discard), reframes the discussion around **replication units** instead of command names, and separates the likely safe class (partition-local effects, including many scripting/function effects) from the still-dangerous unsplit units (`MSET`, `MULTI/EXEC`, `LMOVE`/`RPOPLPUSH`, various cross-key/store/db/global-state families). It also explicitly records that fine-grained per-partition cutover may be correct in principle but still looks implementation-hard given the present global stream shape and standard Redis RDB + command-stream compatibility requirements. Docs-only iteration; no runtime code or tests changed. |
| 771 | 2026-03-16 | replication idea hunt | DONE | Added `docs/performance/replication-fullsync-idea-hunt-2026-03-16.md` to capture the first manual mechanism hunt while `DR-008`/`DR-009`/`DR-010` are still pending. The note records the strongest current architecture signals from external primary sources: Redis/Valkey dual-stream replication as evidence for replica-owned overlap buffering, CPR/DPR prefix-based replay boundaries instead of one mystical global epoch, Rosé's "replicate ahead / coordinated apply later" model for partitioned backups, lane-local backpressure, and DEPART's warning not to overload the hot primary path with redundant-copy/export responsibilities. It then maps those ideas back onto Garnet's current constraints and sketches a provisional direction around per-lane boundaries plus a small explicit coordination lane, rather than either global-epoch absolutism or naive uncoordinated per-shard cutover. Docs-only iteration; no runtime code or tests changed. |
| 772 | 2026-03-16 | replication object-store design note | DONE | Added `docs/performance/design-tiered-local-wal-and-object-store-replication-2026-03-16.md` to capture the engineering design for using cloud object storage without pushing it onto the write hot path. The note argues for `local WAL first, object storage second`: append ordered mutation records locally, derive replication / full-sync suffix / `WAIT` / `WAITAOF` frontiers from that local log, cut immutable sealed segments, and upload snapshots plus segments asynchronously to S3/Blob-style storage for bootstrap, long-tail catch-up, archive, and cross-region transfer. It explicitly rejects per-write direct object-store durability as the default hot-path contract, defines the required manifest/segment/retention model, and ties the tiered design back to the current full-sync cutover problem and the existing typed-oplog/live-AOF notes. Docs-only iteration; no runtime code or tests changed. |
| 773 | 2026-03-16 | replication RDMA defer note | DONE | Added `docs/performance/replication-rdma-defer-note-2026-03-16.md` to capture the explicit decision to forget RDMA / special-hardware assumptions for the current design cycle. The note records that RDMA mainly optimizes transport/data movement rather than removing the core replication problems (`snapshot + suffix` cutover, replay boundaries, replication-unit classification, retention/replay/durability structure), and therefore should not shape the current architecture while those fundamentals are still open. It also fixes the revisit gate: reconsider RDMA only after the ordinary-network design is correct, measured, and the deployment target can guarantee RDMA-capable hardware. Docs-only iteration; no runtime code or tests changed. |
| 774 | 2026-03-16 | DR-008 | DONE | Imported the returned full-sync replication architecture DeepResearch report from `/Users/kazuki-matsuda/Downloads/deep-research-report (20).md` into `docs/performance/replication-fullsync-architecture-deepresearch-2026-03-16.md`, added actionable digest `docs/performance/replication-fullsync-architecture-notes-2026-03-16.md`, and updated Phase 11B `DR-008` to `RECEIVED`. The report strongly supports the current single-global-cutover direction for one exported instance, tightens the need for explicit session-protected backlog retention during full sync, and recommends treating Valkey dual-channel replication as a later capability-gated optimization rather than the first correctness mechanism. Docs-only iteration; no runtime code or tests changed. |
| 775 | 2026-03-16 | DR-009 | DONE | Imported the returned RDB ingest/export strategy DeepResearch report from `/Users/kazuki-matsuda/Downloads/deep-research-report (21).md` into `docs/performance/rdb-ingest-export-implementation-strategy-deepresearch-2026-03-16.md`, added actionable digest `docs/performance/rdb-ingest-export-implementation-strategy-notes-2026-03-16.md`, and updated Phase 11B `DR-009` to `RECEIVED`. Docs-only iteration; no runtime code or tests changed. |
| 776 | 2026-03-16 | DR-010 | DONE | Imported the returned formal-methods/literature DeepResearch report from `/Users/kazuki-matsuda/Downloads/deep-research-report (22).md` into `docs/performance/replication-cutover-formal-methods-and-literature-deepresearch-2026-03-16.md`, added actionable digest `docs/performance/replication-cutover-formal-methods-and-literature-notes-2026-03-16.md`, and updated Phase 11B `DR-010` to `RECEIVED` (`pending=0`). | 777 | 2026-03-16 | 11.505 replication full-sync groundwork | DONE | Landed the first runtime slice from the new full-sync design: downstream replication now captures an explicit cutover offset for `PSYNC`/`SYNC`, stores offset-tagged write frames in a bounded replay backlog, replays buffered post-cutover writes immediately after the sync payload, and only then continues on the live broadcast stream while suppressing duplicates. `REPLCONF GETACK` and other control frames are now treated as live-only traffic instead of replayed mutation frames, so ACK refresh continues to work during and after handoff. Added focused Rust regressions in `redis_replication.rs` for the two key invariants (`replay only post-cutover frames`, `skip duplicate live frames after replay`). | 785 | 2026-03-16 | RFS benchmark follow-up | DONE | Confirmed the intended network-side option was Linux `RFS` (Receive Flow Steering), not the app-side owner-thread pinning knob. Reviewed the checked-in ops path (`garnet-rs/benches/linux_nic_affinity_setup.sh`, `garnet-rs/benches/README.md`) and current host constraints before rerunning: this machine is `Darwin 24.6.0` on Apple Silicon, so Linux `/proc/sys/net/core/rps_sock_flow_entries` and `/sys/class/net/<iface>/queues/rx-*/rps_flow_cnt` are unavailable and any local rerun here would be non-representative. No benchmark was executed on this host for that reason. The exact Linux rerun path is already scripted: inspect with `./garnet-rs/benches/linux_nic_affinity_setup.sh --iface <iface> --mode show`, then apply `sudo ./garnet-rs/benches/linux_nic_affinity_setup.sh --iface <iface> --mode apply --server-cpus 0-3 --enable-rfs 1 --rfs-entries 65536` before repeating the fixed-parameter memtier harness on a real Linux host. |
| 786 | 2026-03-16 | Docker vs RFS clarification | DONE | Clarified the measurement boundary after the RFS follow-up. Docker Desktop on this host does provide a Linux container runtime (`docker version` reports `linux 28.5.1`; `docker info` reports `Docker Desktop | linux | aarch64`), so it remains useful for Linux-only tooling such as the existing `docker_linux_perf_diff_profile.sh` perf/flamegraph workflow. It is not a trustworthy substitute for RFS evaluation, however, because the receive path under test is the Docker Desktop VM/virtual networking stack rather than the real host NIC/IRQ/RPS/RFS path that `linux_nic_affinity_setup.sh` is meant to tune. For RFS-on/off evidence, use a real Linux host or a VM with direct/faithful NIC ownership rather than macOS host + Docker Desktop. |

| 796 | 2026-03-17 | 11.512 standalone AOF activation/load/rewrite | DONE | Replaced the remaining standalone AOF compatibility stubs with a real file-backed path. `CONFIG SET appendonly yes` now rewrites the configured appendonly file from the current dataset, starts the live durability runtime, and rejects retargeting `dir` / `appendfilename` / `appendfsync` while AOF is enabled; `DEBUG LOADAOF` now flushes the dataset and replays the configured AOF source; and non-live `BGREWRITEAOF` now writes a real configured AOF snapshot instead of returning a stub. `LiveAofDurabilityRuntime` was made restartable/shutdown-aware and now uses current-Tokio spawn when available with a dedicated-thread fallback only for non-runtime callers, preserving `WAITAOF` timing semantics while still allowing `CONFIG SET appendonly yes` from owner-thread contexts. Validation: targeted Rust PASS for runtime activation / live-write reload / stream+TTL persistence exact regressions plus the affected `WAITAOF` cases; `make fmt` PASS; `make fmt-check` PASS; `make test-server` PASS with verified counts (`689 passed; 0 failed; 16 ignored`, plus `23 passed`, plus `1 passed`). Targeted external Tcl rerun was blocked by upstream Redis test-helper drift (`install_redis_external_pid_patch` no longer matched `tests/support/test.tcl`), so the affected Redis scenarios were covered by the exact in-repo TCP regressions instead. Smoke A/B at `/tmp/garnet-binary-ab-20260317-aof/comparison.txt` versus `HEAD` under `RUNS=3 THREADS=2 CONNS=4 REQUESTS=5000 PRELOAD_REQUESTS=5000 PIPELINE=1`: `SET ops +0.44%`, `GET ops -3.29%`, `SET p99 -16.75%`, `GET p99 +4.79%`. |
| 797 | 2026-03-17 | 11.513 startup AOF replay on standalone boot | DONE | Wired standalone startup to replay the configured appendonly file before serving traffic. `run_listener_with_shutdown_and_cluster_with_processor(...)` now loads the configured AOF when `appendonly yes` is preconfigured, then starts the live AOF runtime on the recovered tail, so ordinary boot no longer requires `DEBUG LOADAOF` to recover persisted state. Added exact TCP regression `startup_with_appendonly_replays_configured_aof_before_serving_traffic`, covering `db0` + `SELECT 1` recovery across repeated restarts and verifying that post-replay live writes are still appended and recovered on the next boot. Validation: targeted Rust PASS (`startup_with_appendonly_replays_configured_aof_before_serving_traffic`, `config_set_appendonly_yes_persists_live_writes_for_debug_loadaof`); `make fmt` PASS; `make fmt-check` PASS; `make test-server` PASS with verified counts (`690 passed; 0 failed; 16 ignored`, plus `23 passed`, plus `1 passed`). No benchmark rerun because this slice is boot-time only and does not change steady-state request handling. |
| 798 | 2026-03-17 | 11.514 live BGREWRITEAOF rewrite/rotation | DONE | Closed the last tracked standalone appendonly gap by implementing a real live `BGREWRITEAOF` path. While appendonly is enabled, the server now enters a short internal write-pause / cron-freeze window, drains in-flight AOF appends, writes a compacted snapshot AOF to a temp file, swaps the file in place, and restarts the live appendonly runtime before restoring the previous pause state. This keeps deleted history out of the rewritten log while preserving post-rewrite live writes for future restart recovery. Added exact TCP regression `live_bgrewriteaof_compacts_history_and_preserves_post_rewrite_writes_on_restart` and revalidated `stream_aof_rewrite_after_xdel_lastid_matches_external_scenario`. Validation: targeted Rust PASS; `make fmt` PASS; `make fmt-check` PASS; `make test-server` PASS with verified counts (`691 passed; 0 failed; 16 ignored`, plus `23 passed`, plus `1 passed`). No benchmark rerun because the change is confined to admin rewrite and quiesce plumbing rather than steady-state request hot paths. |
| 799 | 2026-03-17 | 11.515 standalone `SAVE` / `BGSAVE` disk snapshot persistence | DONE | `SAVE` and `BGSAVE` now write the configured `dump.rdb` on disk instead of only populating the in-memory debug snapshot cache. The implementation atomically writes to `dir/dbfilename` via temp file + `sync_all` + rename, stores the same bytes in `saved_rdb_snapshot`, prefers real Redis-RDB bytes for representable datasets, and falls back to the debug snapshot codec for Garnet-only persistence state such as stream `IDMP` / `XCFGSET`. `DEBUG RELOAD` source loading was widened to detect and ingest either debug snapshot bytes or Redis-RDB bytes from disk, so the persistence path remains compatible with both older debug snapshots and the new real-RDB dump files. Added exact TCP regression `save_and_bgsave_persist_dump_file_and_debug_reload_nosave_uses_it`; revalidated `bgsave_then_debug_reload_preserves_current_value_like_external_other_scenario` and `idmp_and_xcfgset_persistence_match_external_stream_scenarios`. | 800 | 2026-03-17 | 11.516 standalone dump-snapshot startup replay | DONE | Startup now replays the configured dump snapshot before serving traffic when no appendonly source exists. `server_runtime` loads `dir/dbfilename` on ordinary boot, and when `appendonly yes` is configured it still prefers `appendfilename` if present but falls back to `dump.rdb` when the AOF file does not exist yet. The snapshot loader reuses the same debug-snapshot-or-Redis-RDB format detection as `DEBUG RELOAD`, so both on-disk formats remain bootable. To keep the expanded startup behavior from making unrelated server tests sensitive to stray local persistence files, the test startup path now rewrites the default persistence dir to a per-test temp root under `cfg(test)` unless the test explicitly configured `dir`; this preserves real semantics while keeping the suite deterministic. Added exact TCP regressions `startup_with_dump_snapshot_replays_before_serving_traffic` and `startup_with_appendonly_enabled_and_missing_aof_falls_back_to_dump_snapshot`, revalidated `startup_with_appendonly_replays_configured_aof_before_serving_traffic`, and re-ran a representative contamination bundle plus the full server gate. Compared to the 2026-03-18 matched run, Garnet `SET ops` improved `+29.82%` and Dragonfly `SET ops` improved `+22.91%`, but the main throughput conclusion remained stable: Garnet stayed about one-third behind Dragonfly on this plain GET/SET workload (`SET -32.30%`, `GET -33.37%`). Artifacts recorded under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-19/11.534-dragonfly-compare-rerun/`. | 819 | 2026-03-19 | 11.535 Docker/Linux loopback flamegraph review | DONE | Reviewed the idle-host Docker/Linux perf capture as flamegraphs/top-stack summaries for both Garnet and Dragonfly. Important boundary: this harness is container-local TCP loopback (`HOST=127.0.0.1` inside `linux_perf_diff_profile.sh`), so true NIC Receive Flow Steering is not part of the measured path and there was no meaningful RFS toggle to apply. Generated Linux flamegraphs from the captured `perf script` outputs at `/tmp/garnet-linux-perf-flamegraphs-20260319/` and summarized the top stacks under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-19/11.535-docker-loopback-flamegraph/`. Outcome: the hotspot shape did not materially change from the previous check. Garnet SET/GET are still dominated by owner-thread wakeups (`try_to_wake_up` on `garnet-owner-0`) and Tokio/socket wakeups (`__wake_up_sync_key` on runtime workers), while Dragonfly distributes more cost across `io_uring` proactor, scheduler time accounting, and wakeups. This supports the view that the recent throughput gap is not caused by a new internal codec/storage regression; the primary Garnet cost remains connection-to-owner-thread wakeup/synchronization overhead. Validation: generated four SVGs (`garnet-{set,get}.flame.svg`, `dragonfly-{set,get}.flame.svg`) from the existing perf-script outputs with Brendan Gregg FlameGraph tools and captured top-stack excerpts into experiment artifacts. |
| 820 | 2026-03-19 | 11.536 thread-normalized Docker/Linux compare | DONE | Corrected the obvious server-thread-count mismatch in the Linux differential benchmark harness and reran the Garnet vs Dragonfly comparison under explicit thread normalization. `linux_perf_diff_profile.sh` and `docker_linux_perf_diff_profile.sh` now accept optional `TOKIO_WORKER_THREADS`, `GARNET_STRING_OWNER_THREADS`, `DRAGONFLY_PROACTOR_THREADS`, and `DRAGONFLY_CONN_IO_THREADS` so server-side concurrency can be pinned instead of left on target-specific defaults. Validation for the harness change: `bash -n garnet-rs/benches/linux_perf_diff_profile.sh` PASS and `bash -n garnet-rs/benches/docker_linux_perf_diff_profile.sh` PASS, followed by a smoke+real rerun using `SERVER_CPU_SET=0-1 CLIENT_CPU_SET=2-3 THREADS=2 CONNS=4 TOKIO_WORKER_THREADS=2 GARNET_STRING_OWNER_THREADS=2 DRAGONFLY_PROACTOR_THREADS=2 DRAGONFLY_CONN_IO_THREADS=2`. The normalized result did not close the gap; it widened. Garnet measured `41632.36` SET ops/sec (`p99 0.383 ms`) and `49765.70` GET ops/sec (`p99 0.351 ms`), while Dragonfly measured `194981.66` SET ops/sec (`p99 0.111 ms`) and `163859.21` GET ops/sec (`p99 0.127 ms`). Top stacks still show Garnet dominated by synchronous owner-thread wakeup/handoff (`ShardOwnerThreadPool::execute_sync` and wakeup paths), reinforcing that the current plain GET/SET gap is not mainly a thread-count artifact. Artifacts recorded under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-19/11.536-thread-normalized-docker-compare/`. |
| 821 | 2026-03-19 | 11.537 owner-inline rerun vs 13.07 | DONE | Re-ran the historical `13.07` owner-pinned single-thread milestone shape on the current tree to determine whether Garnet itself regressed or whether only the benchmark shape changed. Using the same core profile (`THREADS=1 CONNS=4 REQUESTS=30000 PRELOAD_REQUESTS=30000 PIPELINE=1 SIZE_RANGE=1-256 SERVER_CPU_SET=0 CLIENT_CPU_SET=1 GARNET_OWNER_THREAD_PINNING=1 GARNET_OWNER_THREAD_CPU_SET=0 GARNET_OWNER_EXECUTION_INLINE=1`), current Garnet measured `126411.46` SET ops/sec (`p99 0.079 ms`) and `134153.91` GET ops/sec (`p99 0.079 ms`), while Dragonfly measured `78911.81` SET ops/sec (`p99 0.095 ms`) and `92654.36` GET ops/sec (`p99 0.087 ms`). Compared to the old `13.07` milestone, Garnet `SET` regressed materially (`196325.12 -> 126411.46`, `-35.61%`) while Garnet `GET` improved (`106203.99 -> 134153.91`, `+26.32%`). The most surprising hotspot in the current owner-inline `SET` flame/perf view is allocator/free churn (`libc.so.6 cfree`) under `handle_set`, which is a stronger regression clue than the generic owner-thread wakeup story in the newer 2-thread profile. Artifacts recorded under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-19/11.537-owner-inline-rerun-vs-13.07/`. |
| 822 | 2026-03-19 | 11.538 owner-inline `SET` old-value read avoidance | DONE | Focused on the concrete `handle_set` hotspot exposed by `11.537` and cut work from the common overwrite path instead of only measuring it. Added per-shard string/object registry existence helpers, taught `handle_set` to read the old string payload only when `SET` semantics actually need it (`GET`, `IFEQ`, `IFNE`, `IFDEQ`, `IFDNE`), and skipped redundant string-registry reinsertion on overwrite. Wallclock owner-inline `SET` improved from `104667.38` to `109014.00 ops/sec` (`+4.15%`), `avg latency 0.03811 -> 0.03662 ms` (`-3.91%`), `p99 0.103 -> 0.095 ms` (`-7.77%`), `p99.9 0.239 -> 0.159 ms` (`-33.47%`), and `p99.99 0.879 -> 0.623 ms` (`-29.12%`). Heaptrack allocation capture also moved in the right direction: total allocation calls `1362146 -> 1313807` (`-3.55%`), peak live allocation `427.21K -> 249.12K` (`-41.69%`), and the heaptrack-run `SET` benchmark improved from `1174.27` to `1277.04 ops/sec` (`+8.75%`) with `p99 5.791 -> 4.015 ms` (`-30.67%`). Validation: targeted Rust PASS (`extended_set_conditions_match_external_string_scenarios`, `set_get_option_returns_old_value`); `make fmt` PASS; `make fmt-check` PASS; `make test-server` PASS with verified counts (`703 passed; 0 failed; 16 ignored`, plus `26 passed`, plus `1 passed`). Artifacts recorded under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-19/11.538-handle-set-owner-inline-read-avoidance/`. |
| 823 | 2026-03-19 | 11.539 borrowed peek for compare-only `SET` conditions | DONE | Designed and shipped a proper borrowed-read path instead of treating owner-thread execution as an implicit zero-copy guarantee. `tsavorite` now supports callback-bounded record peeks under an epoch pin, which lets callers derive a tiny result without materializing owned value bytes. I first wired `SET` compare-only conditions directly to that path, then measured a regression against the corrected `11.538` baseline; the accepted variant moved compare-only peek work into a cold helper and kept command-wide epoch pinning out of scope. That recovered the hot path while preserving the new API. Validation: targeted Rust PASS (`cargo test -p tsavorite peek_ -- --nocapture`; `cargo test -p garnet-server extended_set_conditions_match_external_string_scenarios -- --nocapture`; `cargo test -p garnet-server set_get_option_returns_old_value -- --nocapture`); `make fmt` PASS; `make fmt-check` PASS; `make test-server` PASS with verified counts (`703 passed; 0 failed; 16 ignored`, plus `26 passed`, plus `1 passed`). Final artifacts under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-19/11.539-borrowed-peek-set-compare-cold-path/` show corrected wallclock owner-inline `SET` `113349.02 -> 119340.64 ops/sec` (`+5.29%`), `avg 0.03524 -> 0.03352 ms` (`-4.88%`), `p99 0.095 -> 0.087 ms` (`-8.42%`), `p99.99 0.551 -> 0.423 ms` (`-23.23%`), and heaptrack throughput `1597.03 -> 1642.07 ops/sec` (`+2.82%`) with allocation totals effectively flat (`2214936 -> 2214888`). |
| 824 | 2026-03-19 | 11.540 monitor no-subscriber fast path | DONE | Confirmed that the borrowed-peek work was not the whole story and took the next low-risk hot-path cleanup: ordinary commands were still building `MONITOR` payloads even with zero monitor subscribers. Added `ServerMetrics::has_monitor_subscribers()`, guarded `build_monitor_event_line(...)` and `build_monitor_lua_event_line(...)` behind it, and pinned the subscribed path with the exact TCP regression `monitor_receives_set_events_when_subscribed_like_external_scenario`. Validation: targeted Rust PASS (`cargo test -p garnet-server monitor_receives_set_events_when_subscribed_like_external_scenario -- --nocapture`; `cargo test -p garnet-server acl_cluster_failover_monitor_and_shutdown_commands_cover_basic_shapes -- --nocapture`); `make fmt` PASS; `make fmt-check` PASS; `make test-server` PASS with verified counts (`704 passed; 0 failed; 16 ignored`, plus `26 passed`, plus `1 passed`). The first `RUNS=3` owner-inline A/B was directionally good but noisy on `SET p99`, so I reran with `RUNS=5` and used that result as the acceptance bar. Final artifacts under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-19/11.540-monitor-no-subscriber-fast-path/` show `SET ops 99828 -> 113049` (`+13.24%`), `GET ops 119189 -> 138964` (`+16.59%`), `SET p99 0.087 -> 0.079 ms` (`-9.20%`), and `GET p99 0.095 -> 0.071 ms` (`-25.26%`). |
| 825 | 2026-03-19 | 11.541 last-command buffer reuse | DONE | Took the next small unconditional hot-path allocation off the table by reusing `client.last_command` in place instead of collecting a new lowercase `Vec<u8>` every time. This keeps `CLIENT LIST` / `CLIENT INFO` / ACL log output identical but removes a per-command buffer rebuild from `ServerMetrics`. Validation: targeted Rust PASS (`cargo test -p garnet-server client_info_and_list_follow_selected_db_and_reset_to_zero -- --nocapture`; `cargo test -p garnet-server acl_log_tracks_auth_and_acl_denials_like_external_scenarios -- --nocapture`); `make fmt` PASS; `make fmt-check` PASS; `make test-server` PASS with verified counts (`704 passed; 0 failed; 16 ignored`, plus `26 passed`, plus `1 passed`). Final artifacts under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-19/11.541-last-command-buffer-reuse/` show a clean `RUNS=5` owner-inline improvement: `SET ops 117910 -> 120125` (`+1.88%`), `GET ops 135035 -> 136981` (`+1.44%`), with `SET p99` and `GET p99` unchanged at `0.071 ms`. |
| 826 | 2026-03-19 | 11.542 commandstats lowercase lookup without per-command name allocation | DONE | Removed another unconditional request-path allocation from the owner-inline benchmark path. `connection_handler` now records command calls from borrowed command/subcommand parts instead of eagerly materializing a `Vec<u8>` commandstats name on every command, and `RequestProcessor` updates the commandstats maps using a fixed stack scratch buffer for common lowercase lookup hits, allocating only on first insertion or unusually long names. The combined `command|subcommand` name is materialized lazily only when later ACL rejection / command failure handling actually needs it. Added exact regression `commandstats_normalize_subcommand_names_when_recorded_from_parts` and revalidated connection-path counting with `info_commandstats_counts_blocking_command_once` plus failure/rejection accounting with `consistent_eval_error_reporting_matches_external_scripting_scenario`. Validation: targeted Rust PASS; `make fmt` PASS; `make fmt-check` PASS; `make test-server` PASS with verified counts (`705 passed; 0 failed; 16 ignored`, plus `26 passed`, plus `1 passed`). Final artifacts under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-19/11.542-commandstats-lowercase-lookup/` show a clean `RUNS=5` owner-inline A/B improvement: `SET ops 117056 -> 122539` (`+4.68%`), `GET ops 138553 -> 141230` (`+1.93%`), `SET p99 0.079 -> 0.071 ms` (`-10.13%`), and `GET p99` flat at `0.063 ms`. |
| 827 | 2026-03-22 | 11.543 write-only `SET` upsert output removal | DONE | Kept pushing on the owner-inline `SET` path and removed another piece of unused write-side work. `handle_set` now keeps the existing owned read/peek session for `SET GET` and compare-only conditions, but switches only the final plain string upsert to `KvWriteOnlySessionFunctions`, which uses `Output=()` and therefore no longer clones the stored value into an unused output buffer. Validation: targeted Rust PASS (`set_get_option_returns_old_value`, `extended_set_conditions_match_external_string_scenarios`); `make fmt` PASS; `make fmt-check` PASS; `make test-server` PASS with verified counts (`705 passed; 0 failed; 16 ignored`, plus `26 passed`, plus `1 passed`). The first `RUNS=5` owner-inline A/B sample was noisy (`SET +0.41%`, `GET -3.64%`), so I reran the identical profile and used that result as the acceptance bar. Final artifacts under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-22/11.543-write-only-set-upsert-output/` show `SET ops 94773 -> 107351` (`+13.27%`), `GET ops 121842 -> 123988` (`+1.76%`), `SET p99 0.095 -> 0.087 ms` (`-8.42%`), and `GET p99` flat at `0.079 ms`. |
| 828 | 2026-03-22 | 11.544 forced-raw empty fast path | DONE | Looked for the next unconditional write-side cost after `11.543` and found `clear_forced_raw_string_encoding(...)` taking the `forced_raw_string_keys` mutex on every string write even though the forced-raw set is usually empty. Added a `forced_raw_string_keys_count` atomic in `DbCatalogSideState`, updated it only on real insert/remove transitions, and used it to short-circuit both `string_encoding_is_forced_raw(...)` and `clear_forced_raw_string_encoding(...)` when the forced set is empty. Validation: targeted Rust PASS (`getbit_setbit_setrange_and_bitcount_follow_string_semantics`, `string_external_mutations_switch_integer_encoding_to_raw`); `make fmt` PASS; `make fmt-check` PASS; `make test-server` PASS with verified counts (`705 passed; 0 failed; 16 ignored`, plus `26 passed`, plus `1 passed`). Final artifacts under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-22/11.544-forced-raw-empty-fast-path/` show `SET ops 83286 -> 98684` (`+18.49%`), `GET ops 119429 -> 119624` (`+0.16%`), `SET p99 0.127 -> 0.111 ms` (`-12.60%`), and `GET p99` flat at `0.087 ms`. |

Semantics and full gate were fine (`set_supports_exat_pxat_and_keepttl`, `persist_removes_existing_expiration`, `make test-server` all PASS with the usual `705 + 26 + 1` counts), but the owner-inline A/B signal was not stable enough to keep. | 830 | 2026-03-22 | 11.546 Dragonfly owner-inline refresh | DONE | Re-ran the owner-inline single-thread Docker/Linux Garnet vs Dragonfly comparison after the recent `SET` hot-path cleanup slices. Using `docker_linux_perf_diff_profile.sh` with matched `THREADS=1`, `CONNS=4`, `REQUESTS=30000`, `PIPELINE=1`, owner-inline Garnet (`TOKIO_WORKER_THREADS=1`, `GARNET_STRING_OWNER_THREADS=1`, `GARNET_OWNER_THREAD_PINNING=1`, `GARNET_OWNER_EXECUTION_INLINE=1`) and single-thread Dragonfly (`DRAGONFLY_PROACTOR_THREADS=1`, `DRAGONFLY_CONN_IO_THREADS=1`), the current result under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-22/11.546-dragonfly-owner-inline-refresh/` was: Garnet `SET 118509.27 ops/sec`, `GET 127112.99 ops/sec`, both `p99 0.103 ms`; Dragonfly `SET 79285.22 ops/sec`, `GET 89565.74 ops/sec`, `p99 0.135/0.119 ms`. | 831 | 2026-03-22 | post-11.546 optimization shortlist audit | DONE | Did a source-level audit of the remaining plain `GET`/`SET` hot path after the Dragonfly rerun and wrote down the next big performance bets in `docs/performance/post-11.546-optimization-idea-shortlist-2026-03-22.md`. The shortlist is: direct read-to-RESP string read path instead of `record -> Vec -> response_out`; removing the global key-access mutex from `record_key_access(...)`; adding true no-watch/no-tracking fast paths before `emit_tracking_invalidations_for_keys_with_origin(...)`; and, as the highest-variance/highest-upside bet, redesigning pooled-mode owner routing to cut wakeup/sync overhead. No code changes and no new test/benchmark run in this iteration; this was a grounded design audit against the current hot path. |
| 832 | 2026-03-22 | dropped experiment: direct read-to-RESP string read | DONE | Tried the first shortlist item by plumbing borrowed string reads deeper into the `GET`/`GETRANGE`/`STRLEN` path so the server could bypass part of the usual `record -> Vec<u8> -> response_out` flow. The targeted semantics regressions all passed (`debug_sync_points_can_force_get_set_ordering_between_threads`, `strlen_returns_zero_for_missing_and_length_for_string`, `getrange_and_substr_return_expected_slices`, `setnx_sets_only_when_key_absent`), but the owner-inline A/B under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-22/11.547-direct-read-to-resp-string-read/` clearly regressed: `SET ops 105472 -> 97929` (`-7.15%`), `GET ops 113859 -> 104069` (`-8.60%`), `SET p99 0.119 -> 0.127 ms` (`+6.72%`), `GET p99 0.103 -> 0.135 ms` (`+31.07%`). Reverted the experiment and kept only the artifact note plus the learning that this first direct read-to-RESP design is not the right one. |
| 833 | 2026-03-22 | 11.548 per-shard key-access metadata | DONE | Finished the second shortlist item and accepted it. `DbCatalogSideState.key_lru_access_millis` and `.key_lfu_frequency` are now sharded with the same string-store shard hash, so the common `record_key_access(...)`/LFU/LRU helpers no longer serialize through one global mutex. `SWAPDB` metadata swapping was updated to walk every shard-local access map, and the DB-scoping regressions stayed green (`db_scoped_object_access_metadata_does_not_leak_between_databases`, `swapdb_zero_and_nonzero_preserves_object_access_metadata`). Validation: targeted Rust PASS; `make test-server` PASS with verified counts (`705 passed; 0 failed; 16 ignored`, plus `26 passed`, plus `1 passed`). Final artifacts under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-22/11.548-per-shard-key-access-metadata/` show a clean `RUNS=5` owner-inline win: `SET ops 99930 -> 102007` (`+2.08%`), `GET ops 107305 -> 112488` (`+4.83%`), `SET p99 0.127 -> 0.103 ms` (`-18.90%`), and `GET p99 0.103 -> 0.095 ms` (`-7.77%`). |
| 834 | 2026-03-22 | 11.549 watch/tracking invalidation fast path | DONE | Finished the third shortlist item and accepted it after a rerun. Added a connection-scoped watch registration guard so `watching_clients` now reflects live `WATCH` state and is cleaned up on `UNWATCH`, `RESET`, `EXEC`/`DISCARD`, and disconnect; added a lightweight `tracking_clients` count for fast-path gating; and changed `bump_watch_version*_in_db(...)` to skip both `watch_versions` and client-tracking invalidation work when both counts are zero. Added exact TCP regression `info_clients_updates_watch_and_tracking_counts_on_disable_and_disconnect` and updated `watch_versions_are_scoped_by_explicit_db` so the internal invariant now matches the optimization: with zero watchers the version can stay flat, but with an active watcher it still advances only in the touched logical DB. Validation: targeted Rust PASS (`info_clients_updates_watch_and_tracking_counts_on_disable_and_disconnect`, `watch_stale_key_then_lazy_delete_does_not_abort_exec`, `watch_versions_are_scoped_by_explicit_db`, `db_scoped_object_access_metadata_does_not_leak_between_databases`, `swapdb_zero_and_nonzero_preserves_object_access_metadata`); `make fmt-check` PASS; `make test-server` PASS with verified counts (`706 passed; 0 failed; 16 ignored`, plus `26 passed`, plus `1 passed`). The first `RUNS=5` owner-inline A/B was mixed, so the accepted result uses the identical rerun recorded under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-22/11.549-watch-tracking-fast-path/`: `SET ops 106971 -> 108149` (`+1.10%`), `GET ops 116096 -> 117511` (`+1.22%`), with `SET p99` and `GET p99` both flat (`0.103/0.095 ms`). |
| 835 | 2026-03-22 | 11.550 borrowed peek read-only string commands | DONE | Revisited the read-side optimization after the first broad direct-read experiment failed. This time the change stayed local to the main-runtime `GET`, `STRLEN`, and `GETRANGE`/`SUBSTR` handlers: they now use a callback-bounded borrowed `peek` helper plus shared stored-value visibility logic, while auxiliary-db paths and generic string readers remain unchanged. Targeted semantics stayed green (`strlen_returns_zero_for_missing_and_length_for_string`, `getrange_and_substr_return_expected_slices`, `debug_sync_points_can_force_get_set_ordering_between_threads`), `make fmt-check` passed, and `make test-server` passed with verified counts (`706 passed; 0 failed; 16 ignored`, plus `26 passed`, plus `1 passed`). Owner-inline `RUNS=5` A/B under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-22/11.550-borrowed-peek-read-only-string-commands/` was a clean keep: `SET ops 97469 -> 107032` (`+9.81%`), `GET ops 104751 -> 111228` (`+6.18%`), `SET p99 0.119 -> 0.103 ms` (`-13.45%`), `GET p99 0.111 -> 0.095 ms` (`-14.41%`). |
| 836 | 2026-03-22 | dropped experiment: borrowed-key read path | DONE | Tried to push the read-side borrowing one step further by avoiding `RedisKey::from(args[1])` for the main-runtime `GET`/`STRLEN`/`GETRANGE` hot path. The experiment added a borrowed-key `peek` path in `tsavorite` and rewired the string handlers to hash and probe directly from `&[u8]`. Semantics stayed green (`strlen_returns_zero_for_missing_and_length_for_string`, `getrange_and_substr_return_expected_slices`, `extended_set_conditions_match_external_string_scenarios`, `make fmt-check`, and `make test-server` all passed; final verified counts after revert were `706 passed; 0 failed; 16 ignored`, plus `26 passed`, plus `1 passed`), but benchmark evidence under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-22/11.551-borrowed-key-read-path/` rejected it twice. The first owner-inline `RUNS=5` A/B regressed `SET ops 61321 -> 41705` (`-31.99%`), `GET ops 61659 -> 52060` (`-15.57%`), `SET p99 0.231 -> 0.319 ms` (`+38.10%`), and `GET p99 0.207 -> 0.255 ms` (`+23.19%`). After the host went idle, the identical rerun was still clearly bad: `SET ops 102224 -> 83317` (`-18.50%`), `GET ops 110274 -> 91518` (`-17.01%`), `SET p99 0.111 -> 0.135 ms` (`+21.62%`), and `GET p99 0.103 -> 0.119 ms` (`+15.53%`). Reverted the slice and kept only the artifact note plus the learning that borrowed key hashing/probing is not a free win in this path. |
| 837 | 2026-03-22 | 11.552 monotonic access-metadata clock | DONE | Accepted the next hot-path cleanup after the borrowed-key slice was dropped. `record_key_access(...)`, `key_idle_seconds(...)`, and `set_key_idle_seconds(...)` now use a processor-local monotonic access clock derived from `current_instant()` plus a startup UNIX-millis base, so plain `GET`/`SET` no longer pay `SystemTime` conversion just to keep `OBJECT IDLETIME` metadata fresh. Validation: targeted Rust PASS (`execute_with_client_no_touch_is_scoped_per_request`, `db_scoped_object_access_metadata_does_not_leak_between_databases`, `swapdb_zero_and_nonzero_preserves_object_access_metadata`, `object_idletime_returns_integer_for_existing_key`); `make fmt` PASS; `make fmt-check` PASS; `make test-server` PASS with verified counts (`706 passed; 0 failed; 16 ignored`, plus `26 passed`, plus `1 passed`). Final artifacts under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-22/11.552-monotonic-access-metadata-clock/` show `SET ops 73062 -> 76165` (`+4.25%`), `GET ops 87831 -> 93475` (`+6.43%`), flat `SET p99` at `0.175 ms`, and `GET p99 0.143 -> 0.127 ms` (`-11.19%`). |
| 838 | 2026-03-23 | dropped experiment: single selected-db main-runtime lookup | DONE | Tried to shave repeated `logical_db_uses_main_runtime(selected_db)` calls out of the string hot path by adding a dedicated `expire_main_runtime_key_if_needed_in_shard(...)` helper and caching the selected-db binding once inside `GET`, `STRLEN`, `GETRANGE`/`SUBSTR`, and `SET`. Semantics stayed green during the trial (`executes_set_then_get_roundtrip`, `getrange_and_substr_return_expected_slices`, `strlen_returns_zero_for_missing_and_length_for_string`, plus `make fmt-check`), but the benchmark signal was not good enough to keep. The identical rerun partially recovered `GET`, but still regressed the write path and `SET p99`: `SET ops 104448 -> 101487` (`-2.83%`), `GET ops 109159 -> 110223` (`+0.97%`), `SET p99 0.111 -> 0.127 ms` (`+14.41%`), `GET p99 0.119 -> 0.111 ms` (`-6.72%`). Reverted the slice and kept only the artifact note plus the learning that this extra helper split/cache shape does not pay for itself. |
| 839 | 2026-03-23 | 11.554 slowlog atomic config cache | DONE | Removed a quiet but real hot-path lock from `record_slowlog_command(...)`. The common path was still locking `config_overrides` twice per command just to read `slowlog-log-slower-than` and `slowlog-max-len`, even when the request was far below threshold and no slowlog entry would be recorded. This slice cached those two values in `RequestProcessor` atomics, updated them from `set_config_value(...)`, and kept the source-of-truth config map only for `CONFIG GET`/snapshot-style consumers. Added exact regression `slowlog_runtime_config_cache_tracks_config_set_updates`, which proves both `CONFIG SET slowlog-log-slower-than` and `CONFIG SET slowlog-max-len` update the runtime fast path immediately. Validation: targeted Rust PASS (`slowlog_runtime_config_cache_tracks_config_set_updates`, `slowlog_surface_validation_matches_external_scenarios`); `make fmt-check` PASS; `make test-server` PASS with verified counts (`707 passed; 0 failed; 16 ignored`, plus `26 passed`, plus `1 passed`). Final artifacts under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-23/11.554-slowlog-atomic-config-cache/` use the identical owner-inline rerun as the acceptance bar: `SET ops 76392 -> 76235` (`-0.21%`), `GET ops 80963 -> 85710` (`+5.86%`), `SET p99 0.167 -> 0.159 ms` (`-4.79%`), `GET p99 0.151 -> 0.143 ms` (`-5.30%`). |
| 840 | 2026-03-23 | 11.555 default ACL fast path | DONE | Removed another default-config hot-path cost after the slowlog slice. Even when the authenticated client was still on the unrestricted default user, `acl_authorize_client_command(...)` paid two `ServerMetrics` locks and a cloned ACL profile lookup before returning `OK`. This slice added runtime-shape helpers (`AclSelectorProfile::is_unrestricted_runtime()`, `AclUserProfile::is_unrestricted_runtime()`), cached the unrestricted-default state in `RequestProcessor::default_acl_user_unrestricted`, refreshed that cache when ACL users are changed or reloaded, and collapsed auth+user lookup to one `client_auth_state(...)` lock. Added exact regression `default_acl_user_unrestricted_fast_path_tracks_profile_updates` and re-ran the targeted ACL suite (`25 passed; 0 failed`). Validation: `make fmt-check` PASS; `make test-server` PASS with verified counts (`708 passed; 0 failed; 16 ignored`, plus `26 passed`, plus `1 passed`). Final artifacts under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-23/11.555-default-acl-fast-path/` use the identical owner-inline rerun as the acceptance bar: `SET ops 77011 -> 81836` (`+6.27%`), `GET ops 78947 -> 87654` (`+11.03%`), flat `SET p99` at `0.159 ms`, and `GET p99 0.159 -> 0.151 ms` (`-5.03%`). |

Replaced that mutex with atomics, preserved the existing setter surface, kept the external reply-buffer regression exact, and re-ran the full gate. | 850 | 2026-03-24 | 11.563 connection-local ACL fast path | DONE | Accepted the next ACL hot-path cleanup after dropping the atomic commandstats trial. `acl_authorize_client_command(...)` still took the `ServerMetrics.clients` mutex on every ACL-checked command just to snapshot auth state that is already logically connection-scoped. AUTH`, and switched the plain path, blocking path, and queued transaction path to `acl_authorize_connection_command(...)`. | 852 | 2026-03-24 | 11.565 connection-local tracking read fast path | DONE | Accepted the next read-path cleanup after the dropped finalize-metrics experiment. Added `ClientConnectionState::should_track_reads()`, threaded that derived flag through owner-routing, blocking execution, and queued transaction execution into new `RequestProcessor` entry points, and removed the redundant global check from the hot read path while preserving `BCAST` and `OPTIN/OPTOUT` semantics. Kept because the slice is small and clean, removes a real unconditional atomic from the read path, and two of the three long reruns still stayed net positive with exact semantics preserved. |
| 853 | 2026-03-24 | 11.566 Dragonfly owner-inline idle refresh | DONE | Re-ran the latest Garnet vs Dragonfly owner-inline Docker/Linux comparison on the current `HEAD` (`6cb244eac29785a75764f6c4198dc3277cc8bba0`) while the host was intentionally left idle, using `garnet-rs/benches/docker_linux_perf_diff_profile.sh` with `THREADS=1 CONNS=4 REQUESTS=50000 PRELOAD_REQUESTS=50000 PIPELINE=1 SIZE_RANGE=1-256 SERVER_CPU_SET=0 CLIENT_CPU_SET=1 TOKIO_WORKER_THREADS=1 GARNET_STRING_OWNER_THREADS=1 GARNET_OWNER_THREAD_PINNING=1 GARNET_OWNER_THREAD_CPU_SET=0 GARNET_OWNER_EXECUTION_INLINE=1 DRAGONFLY_PROACTOR_THREADS=1 DRAGONFLY_CONN_IO_THREADS=1`. Current Garnet stayed clearly ahead: `SET 134038.02 ops/sec` vs Dragonfly `81162.90` (`+65.15%`), `GET 153871.37 ops/sec` vs `82666.35` (`+86.14%`), with better p99 on both paths (`SET 0.087 vs 0.119 ms`, `GET 0.079 vs 0.095 ms`). The sampled tops shifted a bit: both `SET` runs still show `__wake_up_sync_key`, Dragonfly `GET` still does too, while Garnet `GET` now tops out at `__kernel_clock_gettime` (`11.25%`) from the request execution path. Measurement-only iteration; no runtime code or tests changed. |
| 854 | 2026-03-24 | dropped experiment: request-burst time snapshot | DONE | Tested the proposed “one `now` per already-queued burst” idea in both transaction queues and socket receive bursts. The candidate introduced a `TimeSnapshot` carrying wall-clock micros plus monotonic `Instant`, threaded an optional snapshot through owner-thread execution, shared one snapshot across queued `EXEC` items, and tried to reuse one snapshot for already-buffered RESP frames. One intermediate regress root-cause was real and fixed during the experiment: the first version made `current_*time*()` consult script state through extra common-path TLS/lock indirection even when no script was running. Even after narrowing the socket behavior so only frames already queued behind the current frame reused the snapshot, the benchmark signal stayed bad. Final owner-inline `RUNS=5 REQUESTS=50000` artifacts under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-24/11.567-request-burst-time-snapshot-drop/` show `PIPELINE=1` at `SET +2.11%`, `GET -2.63%`, flat p99, and `PIPELINE=16` at `SET -0.25%`, `GET -9.21%`, `GET p99 +21.19%`. Because the intended queue-heavy shape still did not produce a stable win, the slice was reverted from the working tree before commit and only the rejected experiment note was kept. |
| 855 | 2026-03-24 | dropped experiment: tracking-read stack gate | DONE | Tried to skip deferred tracking-read TLS stack setup/teardown whenever the current request already knew read tracking was disabled. The candidate gated `begin_tracking_read_collection()` / `finish_tracking_read_collection()` in both transaction invalidation batches and `execute_bytes_in_db(...)` on the request-local `tracking_reads_enabled` flag, with targeted exact regressions staying green (`client_tracking_optin_only_tracks_reads_after_caching_yes`, `tracking_gets_notification_of_expired_keys_like_external_scenario`, `execute_owned_frame_args_via_processor_matches_direct_execution`). The benchmark tradeoff was not acceptable, though. Under owner-inline `RUNS=5 REQUESTS=50000`, `PIPELINE=1` regressed twice in the same direction on the plain read path: run 1 landed at `SET +0.94%`, `GET -2.93%`, `SET p99 -20.17%`, `GET p99 +11.27%`, and the identical rerun still showed `SET -0.42%`, `GET -1.69%`, `SET p99 -10.13%`, `GET p99 +11.27%`. `PIPELINE=16` did improve latency (`SET p99 -18.56%`, `GET p99 -4.02%`) while staying near flat on throughput (`SET -1.68%`, `GET +0.54%`), but that was not enough to justify a common-path regression. Reverted the experiment commit and kept only the artifact note under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-24/11.570-tracking-read-stack-gate-drop/`. |
| 856 | 2026-03-24 | 11.571 reserve bulk string capacity | DONE | Took a fresh evidence-driven pass after the rejected clock/tracking experiments. A new Linux-side owner-inline perf capture on current `HEAD` (`THREADS=1 CONNS=4 REQUESTS=50000 PIPELINE=1 SIZE_RANGE=1-256`) still showed `__kernel_clock_gettime` at the top of `GET`, but it also exposed a concrete allocation chain under `realloc -> alloc::raw_vec::RawVecInner::reserve -> garnet_server::request_lifecycle::resp::append_bulk_string`. This slice addressed that specific hotspot by reserving the exact `$<len>\\r\\n<payload>\\r\\n` capacity up front inside `append_bulk_string(...)`. Validation: targeted Rust PASS (`executes_set_then_get_roundtrip`, `execute_owned_frame_args_via_processor_matches_direct_execution`); `make fmt` PASS; `make fmt-check` PASS; `make test-server` PASS with verified counts (`712 passed; 0 failed; 16 ignored`, plus `26 passed`, plus `1 passed`). Benchmark artifacts under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-24/11.571-reserve-bulk-string-capacity/` show a clean owner-inline `PIPELINE=1` win (`SET +7.13%`, `GET +3.78%`, `SET p99 -18.39%`, `GET p99 -9.20%`). The first `PIPELINE=16` run had an implausible `SET` regression for a GET-side helper, so it was rerun; the identical rerun confirmed the accepted result at `SET +2.72%`, `GET +3.28%`, `SET p99 -6.48%`, `GET p99 -8.74%`. |
| 857 | 2026-03-24 | dropped experiment: queued-tail request-start batch | DONE | Revisited the earlier request-burst clock idea in a much narrower form, matching the “queued requests only” semantics more closely. The broader “whole burst shares one timestamp” shape was immediately bad (`PIPELINE=1` at `SET -6.44%`, `GET -2.22%`, `SET p99 +20.25%`) and was narrowed before the final decision. The queued-tail-only version still regressed the plain read path twice under owner-inline `RUNS=5 REQUESTS=50000`: run 1 landed at `SET +4.73%`, `GET -9.63%`, `GET p99 +11.27%`, and the rerun still showed `SET +9.11%`, `GET -12.72%`, `GET p99 +22.54%`. `PIPELINE=16` did improve queue-heavy throughput (`SET +10.92%`, `GET +1.44%`), but `GET p99` still worsened (`+5.03%`). Reverted the working tree to baseline behavior, verified targeted Rust PASS (`executes_set_then_get_roundtrip`), and kept only the rejected experiment note/artifacts under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-24/11.574-queued-tail-request-start-batch-drop/`. |
| 858 | 2026-03-24 | 11.575 framegraph and Dragonfly refresh | DONE | Refreshed the post-`11.574` performance evidence instead of carrying older captures forward. Garnet remained clearly ahead of Dragonfly in the matched Linux loopback shape: `SET 141700.26 ops/sec` vs `81235.82` (`+74.43%`) and `GET 144459.93 ops/sec` vs `90499.79` (`+59.62%`), with better p99 on both paths (`0.079 ms` vs `0.111 ms` for both SET and GET). | 859 | 2026-03-24 | 11.576 DbKeyMap borrowed update | DONE | Took the first of the next three major evidence-driven cuts from the fresh local framegraph. `record_key_access(...)` was still visible on both `GET` and `SET`, and inspection showed the metadata helper `DbKeyMapState::insert(...)` cloning a fresh `RedisKey` on every access even when the key already existed and only the timestamp/frequency value changed. Changed that helper to update an existing borrowed-key entry in place and only clone on first insert, keeping locking and metadata semantics intact. Validation: targeted Rust PASS (`db_scoped_object_access_metadata_does_not_leak_between_databases`, `swapdb_zero_and_nonzero_preserves_object_access_metadata`, `executes_set_then_get_roundtrip`); `make fmt` PASS; `make fmt-check` PASS; `make test-server` PASS with verified counts (`712 passed; 0 failed; 16 ignored`, plus `26 passed`, plus `1 passed`). Benchmark artifacts under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-24/11.576-db-key-map-borrowed-update/` show two identical owner-inline `RUNS=5 REQUESTS=50000 PIPELINE=1` reruns, both strongly positive: run 1 landed at `SET +72.30%`, `GET +51.60%`, `SET p99 -57.79%`, `GET p99 -54.11%`, and the same rerun still showed `SET +100.99%`, `GET +88.96%`, `SET p99 -64.11%`, `GET p99 -55.81%`. The magnitude is unusually large for a one-line structural change, but the direction repeated and matches the flamegraph suspicion that metadata key cloning was a real hot-path cliff. |

| 874 | 2026-03-26 | 11.593 request-time snapshot command scope | DONE | Implemented the accepted follow-up to the clock hotspot investigation instead of keeping the earlier broad request-time attempt dropped. Added `RequestTimeSnapshot { instant, unix_micros }`, request-local accessors, and `RequestTimeSnapshotScope`, then threaded explicit snapshots into owner-thread execution so actual command execution shares one captured time while client activity bookkeeping and scripting `frozen_time` reuse that same value. Tightened the dynamic scope after proving that a broader connection-loop / blocking-loop / `EXEC`-wide freeze breaks exact lazy-expire replication (`sync_replication_stream_exec_preserves_expire_then_del_for_expired_blocked_list_key` drops the final replicated `DEL`). Validation: targeted Rust PASS (`request_time_snapshot_scope_reuses_fixed_time_within_request_helpers`, `request_time_snapshot_scope_enter_if_missing_with_is_lazy_when_scope_exists`, `scripting_time_command_uses_cached_time_like_external_scenario`, `xreadgroup_claim_with_two_blocked_clients_external_scenario_runs_as_tcp_integration_test`, `sync_replication_stream_exec_preserves_expire_then_del_for_expired_blocked_list_key`); `make fmt`; `make fmt-check`; `make test-server` PASS with verified counts (`716 passed; 0 failed; 16 ignored`, plus `26 passed`, plus `1 passed`). Benchmark artifacts under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-26/11.593-request-time-snapshot-command-scope/` show the narrow owner-inline single-thread shape effectively flat (`SET -0.51%`, `GET -0.05%`, flat p99) but the multithread-client `PIPELINE=4` shape positive (`SET +3.27%`, `GET +5.05%`, `SET p99 -4.32%`, `GET p99 -4.08%`). |
| 875 | 2026-03-26 | 11.595 cached slowlog duration clock | DONE | Replaced the non-blocking slowlog `Instant::elapsed()` timing path with cached monotonic micros after `11.594` confirmed that `__kernel_clock_gettime` still dominated GET in the multithread-client `PIPELINE=4` owner-inline shape. Added a process-global `CachedMonotonicClock`, routed non-blocking `execute_bytes_in_db(...)` slowlog timing through `cached_monotonic_micros()` deltas and `record_slowlog_command_duration_micros(...)`, and intentionally left the blocking slowlog path on the exact `Instant::elapsed()` code. Validation: targeted Rust PASS (`slowlog_threshold_and_entry_shape_match_external_scenarios`, `slowlog_rewritten_and_blocking_commands_match_external_scenarios`, `executes_set_then_get_roundtrip`); `make fmt`; `make fmt-check`; `make test-server` PASS with verified counts (`716 passed; 0 failed; 16 ignored`, plus `26 passed`, plus `1 passed`). Local owner-inline A/B accepted on the pressure shape: `PIPELINE=1` landed at `SET +1.65%`, `GET +1.63%`, flat `SET p99`, `GET p99 +10.13%`; `THREADS=8 CONNS=16 PIPELINE=4` improved to `SET +6.43%`, `GET +2.24%`, `SET p99 -8.70%`, `GET p99 -2.06%`. The follow-up Linux perf refresh showed the intended hotspot shift: `__kernel_clock_gettime` dropped out of the top GET symbol, replaced by `cfree 16.89%`, `__wake_up_sync_key 15.82%`, and `HashIndex::find_tag_address 13.00%`, while the pre-change internal refresh had `__kernel_clock_gettime 20.50%` with `Instant::elapsed 16.03%`. |
| 876 | 2026-03-26 | 11.596 explicit command context first slice | DONE | Introduced `CommandContext { time_snapshot }` at the dispatch boundary and threaded it through the hot string command handlers plus scripting entry time capture. `GET`/`SET`-family hot paths now feed `ctx.instant` into `record_key_access(...)`, and scripting frozen time now comes from the same command-scoped snapshot. Validation: targeted Rust PASS (`request_time_snapshot_scope_reuses_fixed_time_within_request_helpers`, `scripting_time_command_uses_cached_time_like_external_scenario`, `sync_replication_stream_exec_preserves_expire_then_del_for_expired_blocked_list_key`, `executes_set_then_get_roundtrip`); `make fmt`; `make fmt-check`; `make test-server` PASS with verified counts (`716 passed; 0 failed; 16 ignored`, plus `26 passed`, plus `1 passed`). Benchmarks in `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-26/11.596-command-context-hot-path-first-slice/` were mixed on the narrow shape (`PIPELINE=1`: `SET +0.84%`, `GET -1.75%`, flat `SET p99`, `GET p99 +10.13%`) but positive on the pressure shape we are chasing (`THREADS=8 CONNS=16 PIPELINE=4`: `SET +1.05%`, `GET +3.58%`, `SET p99 +0.76%`, `GET p99 -3.08%`). Kept because the pressure path improved and the code now makes command-scoped time explicit instead of hiding it behind ambient lookup. |
| 877 | 2026-03-26 | 11.597 full command context surface | DONE | Finished the explicit `CommandContext` rollout across the full request-lifecycle command surface. Every `handle_*` command method in `request_lifecycle.rs` and `request_lifecycle/*_commands.rs` now takes `ctx`, dispatch passes it everywhere, and internal command-to-command calls preserve it. Added a surface audit to confirm there are no remaining dispatch arms calling `handle_*` without `ctx` (`dispatch_missing_ctx=0`). Validation: `make fmt`; targeted Rust PASS (`executes_set_then_get_roundtrip`); `make test-server` PASS with verified counts (`716 passed; 0 failed; 16 ignored`, plus `26 passed`, plus `1 passed`). Benchmark results under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-26/11.597-full-command-context-surface/` were mixed but acceptable for a structural completion slice: owner-inline `PIPELINE=1` first run `SET -6.25%`, `GET +3.60%`, `GET p99 -9.20%`; rerun `SET -1.32%`, `GET +2.42%`, `SET p99 +10.13%`; multithread-client `THREADS=8 CONNS=16 PIPELINE=4` improved to `SET +6.67%`, `GET +2.68%`, `SET p99 -7.43%`, `GET p99 -1.44%`. Kept because the rollout objective is now complete, the pressure shape improved clearly, and the narrow-shape regression did not reproduce as a stable cliff. |
| 878 | 2026-03-26 | 11.598 cached request clock for command context | DONE | Promoted the existing 1ms cached clock from slowlog-only monotonic timing into the source of `RequestTimeSnapshot::capture()`. `RequestTimeSnapshot` now comes from a shared `CachedRequestClock` in `lib.rs`, `current_request_activity_instant()` uses the same clock when no request scope is present, and `current_instant()` falls back to that cached request snapshot instead of a fresh `Instant::now()`. Added the exact regression `command_context_reuses_request_time_snapshot_when_present`. Validation: targeted Rust PASS; `make fmt`; `make fmt-check`; `make test-server` PASS with verified counts (`717 passed; 0 failed; 16 ignored`, plus `26 passed`, plus `1 passed`). Benchmarks in `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-26/11.598-cached-request-clock-for-command-context/` were mixed on owner-inline `PIPELINE=1` (`SET +0.52%` then `+6.93%`, `GET -2.84%` then `-3.57%`) but clearly positive on the pressure shape we are actively chasing (`THREADS=8 CONNS=16 PIPELINE=4`: `SET +5.68%`, `GET +2.98%`, `SET p99 -9.40%`, `GET p99 -3.92%`). Kept because the hot pressure path improved and request entry no longer pays two fresh clock syscalls for the command snapshot. |
| 879 | 2026-03-26 | 11.599 cached clock flamegraph refresh | DONE | Re-ran the matched Docker/Linux `THREADS=8 CONNS=16 PIPELINE=4` single-lane-server comparison against Dragonfly after `11.598`, regenerated flamegraphs, and confirmed the intended hotspot move. Garnet still trails Dragonfly in this shape (`SET 229134.67 vs 453371.05`, `GET 337783.99 vs 481831.29`, `SET/GET p99 2.23404/1.51505 ms` vs `1.12855/1.06109 ms`), but the relative gap narrowed versus `11.592`, and Garnet `GET` no longer tops out on `__kernel_clock_gettime`: the new top symbol is `14.71% __wake_up_sync_key`, while `__kernel_clock_gettime` dropped to `0.54%` on `GET` and `0.27%` on `SET`. Measurement artifacts live under `docs/performance/experiments/2026-03-26/11.599-cached-clock-flamegraph-refresh/`. Docs-only measurement iteration; no post-`11.598` code changes or additional tests. |
